vulnerabilities.title: Excess ETH sent through PhiNFT1155 Claimable paths is permanently trapped in art contracts
vulnerabilities.summary: `Claimable.signatureClaim()` and `Claimable.merkleClaim()` on `PhiNFT1155` forward user ETH to `PhiFactory`. In `PhiFactory._processClaim`, excess ETH is refunded to `_msgSender()`. For these forwarded calls, `_msgSender()` is the `PhiNFT1155` contract address. Because `PhiNFT1155` has a payable `receive()` but no withdrawal path, any overpayment is retained in the NFT contract and cannot be recovered by the original payer.

description.desc: _processClaim refunds excess payment to `_msgSender()`; when claims are forwarded via `PhiNFT1155` (Claimable), `_msgSender()` is the NFT contract, not the end user.

vulnerabilities.impact: Permanent loss of user ETH overpayments on the NFT-contract claim path.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A user calls `PhiNFT1155.signatureClaim()` or `PhiNFT1155.merkleClaim()` and sends `msg.value > required mint fee` (for example, adding a slippage buffer). 2. `Claimable` forwards that ETH to `PhiFactory.signatureClaim/merkleClaim`. 3. `_processClaim` computes the exact mint fee and executes the refund to `_msgSender()` (the NFT contract). 4. Refunded ETH lands in `PhiNFT1155.receive()` and remains stuck, since no withdrawal function exists for that balance. 5. The user permanently loses the excess amount.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Refund to the original payer instead of `msg.sender` for forwarded claim flows (pass payer address through claim parameters and use it in `_processClaim`), and/or enforce exact payment in `Claimable` (`msg.value == getArtMintFee(...)`) so excess cannot be trapped.

vulnerabilities.title: createArt signatures do not bind caller or CreateConfig, enabling unauthorized reward-recipient hijacking
vulnerabilities.summary: `PhiFactory.createArt` accepts `(signedData_, signature_, createConfig_)`, but `_validateArtCreationSignature` only checks `keccak256(signedData_)`. Critical fields (`artist`, `receiver`, `mintFee`, `maxSupply`, start/end times, soulbound flag) are not cryptographically bound to the signature, and `msg.sender` is not bound either. Any party who obtains a valid creation signature can submit it with attacker-chosen config values and create unauthorized art entries that route mint-side rewards to a

description.desc: Signature verification authenticates only `signedData_` (`expiresIn`, `uri`, `credData`), while all economic/admin creation fields in `CreateConfig` and caller identity are unsigned and attacker-controlled.

vulnerabilities.impact: Unauthorized parties can divert mint-side value from intended recipients and monetize leaked/mempool-exposed creation signatures.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A legitimate creator obtains a valid `signedData_` + `signature_` for a new art and broadcasts `createArt`. 2. An attacker copies the same signed payload and front-runs with `createConfig_.receiver` (and optionally `artist`) set to attacker-controlled addresses. 3. Contract accepts attacker tx because only `signedData_` is verified. 4. The attacker-controlled art is now live and receives mint-side economic flows (`mintFee + artistReward`, plus optional referral routing), while the signature owner intended a different receiver configuration.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Sign and verify all security-critical creation parameters and intended submitter (e.g., EIP-712 struct including `CreateConfig`, caller, chainId, contract address, nonce). Reject signature reuse via nonce/used-hash tracking.
